one_line_takeaway: WILDREWARD shows that a reward model trained via ordinal regression directly on automatically-mined, noise-filtered WildChat user feedback (186k instances, no preference pairs) matches or beats conventional Bradley-Terry reward models on RewardBench/RM-Bench/PPE/JudgeBench, with markedly better calibration (ECE 2.76% vs 8.81%) and cross-sample ROC-AUC (0.79 vs 0.47-0.56), and improves online DPO downstream performance (58.1->62.6 average).
key_finding: On reward-model benchmarks (Table 1), WILDREWARD-8B scores RewardBench 86.0, RM-Bench Easy/Normal/Hard 83.5/78.4/69.7, PPE Human/Correctness 62.5/65.6, JudgeBench 66.0 -- comparable to or exceeding much larger 70B baselines on some columns (e.g., RM-Bench Hard: WILDREWARD-8B 69.7 vs. best baseline INF-ORM-Llama3.1-70B 54.0; PPE Correctness: WILDREWARD-8B 65.6 vs. baselines' 60.6-64.4) despite using no human-annotated preference pairs. Ablation (Table 2, RM-Bench Normal) shows removing Refusal Validation drops the Safety-Refusal (SRF) subset score from 90.4 to 28.5 (a ~68% relative drop) despite the strategy fixing only ~572 label errors; removing Implicit Feedback Mining drops SRF to 68.3 and Chat to 77.3. Calibration: WILDREWARD achieves ECE 2.76% vs. ArmoRM-Llama3-8B-v0.1's 8.81%; thresholding score margin at 0.2 raises accuracy to 87% while retaining ~50% of predictions. Cross-sample consistency (948-instance held-out set): WildReward-4B ROC-AUC 0.79 vs. ArmoRM-Llama3-8B 0.47, Athene-RM-8B 0.56, Skywork-Reward-Llama-3.1-8B-v0.2 0.53. DPO application (Table 3): Online DPO with WILDREWARD raises Llama3.1-8B-Instruct average from 58.1 to 62.6 (vs. Online DPO with ArmoRM at 60.4), with the largest gains on Alpaca Eval 2.0 (33.1->41.3) and Arena Hard (55.7->63.5); Offline DPO with WILDREWARD yields almost no improvement (58.1->58.6).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is: (1) WILDFB, a 186k-instance dataset built by an automated pipeline that classifies WildChat human-LLM follow-up queries into five satisfaction levels (Explicit Rejection, Error Correction, Neutral Ambiguity, Positive Engagement, Explicit Satisfaction) using gpt-oss-120b, then applies two refinement stages -- implicit feedback mining (using all-MiniLM-L6-v2 sentence-embedding cosine similarity >0.6 within a two-turn window to reclassify ~12,310 Neutral Ambiguity instances as positive, a 29% expansion of the positive subset) and refusal validation (using gpt-oss-120b to flag ~572 cases where negative feedback followed a justified safety refusal); and (2) WILDREWARD, a reward model (Qwen3-4B/8B backbones) trained directly on this point-wise, four-level ordinal feedback via an ordinal-regression objective (cumulative-logit style loss over K-1=3 thresholds) rather than the conventional Bradley-Terry pairwise-preference objective, producing a continuous reward score R(x) = 1 + sum of P(y>k).
threat_model: Not applicable -- this is a reward-modeling/alignment-methodology paper, not a security paper. It defines no adversary, attack surface, or defense. The only security-adjacent language appears in the Ethical Considerations section as a one-sentence usage caveat: 'WILDREWARD is trained on real-world data and may inherently contain biases. Users should not exploit these biases for malicious purposes, such as reward hacking. We advise that the usage of WILDREWARD should undergo a verification process before usage.' This is a boilerplate disclaimer, not a studied threat model, and should not be treated as the paper's contribution.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Limitations section): (1) WILDFB covers only English and Chinese WildChat conversations, limiting multilingual applicability. (2) No systematic hyperparameter/backbone search was performed for WILDREWARD due to compute constraints, so reported results may be suboptimal. (3) WILDREWARD was not used for full RL training (only DPO) because RL training is resource-intensive and hard to stabilize. Additional caveats visible in the text: the feedback-quality classification relies on a single LLM judge (gpt-oss-120b) as both labeler and quality-verifier, evaluated for reliability only via a 200-instance manual spot check; the refusal-validation ablation shows the safety-relevant signal is derived from an unusually small correction set (572 instances), raising fragility concerns about how much of the safety benefit generalizes beyond WildChat-style refusal patterns; and online DPO training used only ~20k prompts.

datasets_used: WildChat (human-LLM conversation source; ODC-By license) -- 10,000-instance preliminary sample and full pipeline source
WILDFB (this paper's constructed dataset: 186k instances across 4 feedback categories, English+Chinese)
RM-Bench (Easy/Normal/Hard difficulty levels; Chat/Math/Code/Safety-Refusal/Safety-Response subsets used in ablation)
948-instance curated held-out WildChat binary evaluation set (for ROC-AUC cross-sample consistency)
Infinity-Instruct (20,000 prompts used for DPO training, CC BY-NC 4.0 license)
GSM8K, MATH-500, MMLU Pro, IFEval, Alpaca Eval 2.0, Arena Hard (DPO downstream evaluation benchmarks)
quantitative_results: WILDREWARD-8B: RewardBench 86.0, RM-Bench Easy 83.5 / Normal 78.4 / Hard 69.7, PPE Human 62.5 / Correctness 65.6, JudgeBench 66.0 (Table 1)
WILDREWARD-4B: RewardBench 83.6, RM-Bench Easy 82.0 / Normal 77.0 / Hard 68.6, PPE Human 61.6 / Correctness 63.6, JudgeBench 61.1 (Table 1)
Best 70B baseline INF-ORM-Llama3.1-70B: RewardBench 95.1, RM-Bench Hard 54.0, PPE Correctness 64.4, JudgeBench 70.2 (Table 1)
Ablation on RM-Bench Normal, SRF subset: full WILDREWARD-4B 90.4, w/o Feedback Mining 68.3, w/o Refusal Validation 28.5, w/o All 39.7 (Table 2)
Refusal Validation strategy corrects ~572 mislabeled instances out of 186k
Implicit feedback mining reclassifies ~12,310 additional instances, expanding the positive-feedback subset by 29%
Score-margin threshold 0.2 -> accuracy 87% at ~50% data coverage
ROC-AUC on 948-instance held-out set: WildReward-4B 0.79, ArmoRM-Llama3-8B 0.47, Athene-RM-8B 0.56, Skywork-Reward-Llama-3.1-8B-v0.2 0.53 (Figure 5)
DPO downstream average (Table 3): base Llama3.1-8B-Instruct 58.1, Offline DPO w/ WILDREWARD 58.6, Online DPO w/ ArmoRM 60.4, Online DPO w/ WILDREWARD 62.6
Online DPO w/ WILDREWARD per-task gains: GSM8K 83.6->87.9, MATH-500 49.6->51.6, IFEval 78.7->82.1, Alpaca Eval 2.0 33.1->41.3, Arena Hard 55.7->63.5, MMLU Pro 48.0->48.9

Preliminary WildChat analysis: 82% of follow-up queries Neutral, 17% Negative, 1% Positive (10,000-sample); five-way taxonomy distribution: Explicit Rejection 3%, Error Correction 14%, Neutral Ambiguity 76%, Positive Engagement 6%, Explicit Satisfaction 1%
